metadata.notes: Wingspan standard overhaul: removed legacy sections format, added rules_citations with key rulings, validated content against official rulebook.

subtopics.title: Components & Materials
subtopics.content:
#### Board & Shared Components
- 1 double-sided game board with 6 charter areas (sections) surrounding a central commons area
- 1 index with 36 numbered cards stored in a card archive box
- 6 charter boxes (one per player/charter), each sealed with unique starting stickers and components
- 1 progress track board for tracking game-to-game campaign progress
- Crate tokens (numbered containers that unlock new content when opened)

#### Per-Player Components
- 2 wooden worker meeples in your charter's color
- 1 wooden VP marker
- 1 wooden reputation marker
- 1 wooden influence token
- Starting resource cards and coins
- A charter box containing your unique starting stickers

#### Legacy Materials
- 30+ minion tokens (generic workers for unoccupied charters)
- Sealed tuck boxes and card packs revealed as the campaign progresses
- Sticker sheets that permanently modify the game board
- The game board itself evolves — buildings you construct are stickered onto the board permanently

subtopics.title: First Game Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Before Your First Game
1. Punch out all tokens and sort them into labeled bags
2. Place the game board in the center of the table (use Side 1 for the campaign)
3. Each player selects a charter (1–6) and takes the matching charter box
4. Open your charter box — apply only the stickers marked "Game 1" to your charter area
5. Place 1 worker on your charter's starting building
6. Keep your second worker in reserve

#### Initial Resources
- Each player starts with a set of basic resources (coins, pumpkins, etc.) as shown on their charter card
- Place your VP marker on 10 on the score track
- Place your reputation marker on the center of the reputation track
- Unused charters get 2 minion workers placed on their starting buildings

#### Important First-Game Notes
- Do NOT open any sealed content until instructed
- Read the campaign guide's "Game 1" section aloud
- The index cards (numbered 1–36) are referenced throughout — only look up a card when directed

subtopics.title: Game Structure & Flow
subtopics.content:
#### Campaign Structure
Charterstone is a 12-game legacy campaign. Each game takes 45–75 minutes. Between games, new content is unlocked, buildings are permanently added to the board, and the village evolves based on player decisions.

#### Single Game Flow
Each game follows this pattern:
1. **Setup**: Apply any new stickers, read the campaign guide entry, set starting resources
2. **Play**: Take turns placing workers on buildings to gain resources, construct new buildings, and advance objectives
3. **Trigger End**: The game ends when a set number of buildings have been constructed or a specific condition is met
4. **Score**: Tally VP from buildings, reputation, influence, guideposts (objectives), and special bonuses
5. **Between Games**: Open crates earned, apply permanent changes, record scores on the campaign progress track

#### Player Interaction
- You can place workers in ANY charter area, not just your own
- Building in another player's charter gives them a small bonus
- The commons area has neutral buildings anyone can use
- Reputation and influence tracks create indirect competition

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Turn Structure
On your turn, you must do ONE of the following:
1. **Place a worker** on an empty building space to activate that building's ability
2. **Construct a building** by paying the required resources, then place a building sticker permanently on an empty construction site in your charter
3. **Return all your workers** from the board back to your supply (if all your workers are already placed)

Play continues clockwise until the game-end condition is met.

#### The Engine Loop
The core loop works like this: Place workers on buildings to gain resources or effects -> Use resources to construct new buildings in your charter -> New buildings give you more powerful placement options -> Repeat, building an increasingly efficient engine over the course of each game and across the entire 12-game campaign.

#### Placing Workers
- You may place a worker on ANY building on the entire board, not just buildings in your own charter
- Each building space can hold only 1 worker at a time
- When you place on a building, you immediately resolve that building's effect (gain resources, advance on a track, score VP, etc.)
- If the building is in another player's charter, the charter owner gains a small bonus (typically 1 VP or 1 coin)
- You may also place a worker on buildings in the central commons area

#### Constructing Buildings
- Pay the resource cost shown on a building card in your hand
- Peel the matching building sticker and place it permanently on an empty construction site in YOUR charter area
- You immediately gain VP and may gain bonus effects
- The new building is available for all players to use on future turns
- Construction is the primary way to develop your charter and increase your options

#### Returning Workers
- If all your workers are on the board (or you choose to), you may take them all back to your personal supply
- This is your only "pass" action — it costs your full turn but replenishes your workers
- Timing your worker returns is an important tactical decision

#### Key Concept: Charter Ownership
Each player owns one of the 6 charter areas on the board. Buildings you construct go in YOUR charter. When other players use buildings in your charter, you benefit. Over the campaign, your charter becomes your personalized engine.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Resource System
- **Starting resources**: Coins, pumpkins, coal, wood, and iron are available from Game 1
- Additional resource types are introduced through crate openings as the campaign progresses
- Resources are gained by placing workers on production buildings or through building effects
- Resources are spent primarily to construct buildings and to fulfill guidepost objectives
- There is no hand limit on resources; store them in your charter chest between games

#### The Reputation Track
- A linear track running along the edge of the board
- Advancing on the reputation track earns VP at certain thresholds
- Your position on the reputation track may determine turn order advantages and tiebreakers
- Some buildings and guideposts reward or require reputation advancement
- Reputation position carries over between games in the campaign (with some reset)

#### The Influence Track
- Influence measures your long-term progress and investment in the village
- Gained primarily through constructing buildings and completing guideposts
- Influence determines the overall campaign winner across all 12 games
- At the end of each game, your score contributes to your cumulative influence total
- The player with the most influence at the end of Game 12 wins the campaign

#### Guideposts (Objectives)
- Each game has a set of guidepost cards that provide objectives for players to complete
- Guideposts drive the story forward — completing them triggers crate openings and narrative events
- When you complete a guidepost, you gain VP and potentially unlock new content
- Guideposts vary each game and are thematically tied to the campaign's narrative
- Some guideposts are competitive (first to complete gets a bonus), others are available to all

#### Crate System
- Crates are numbered sealed cards stored in the index card archive
- When a crate number is triggered (by guideposts, certain buildings, or game events), the group opens that crate
- Crates contain: new building stickers, new rule cards, new resources, persona cards, story elements, and sometimes entirely new mechanics
- Once a crate is opened, its contents are permanent — new rules stay in effect for all future games
- The crate system is the heart of the legacy experience, introducing surprises and evolving the game

#### Building Types
- **Production buildings**: Gain resources when a worker is placed
- **Conversion buildings**: Trade one set of resources for another
- **VP buildings**: Score victory points directly
- **Track buildings**: Advance on the reputation or influence tracks
- **Special buildings**: Unique effects introduced through crates (varies by campaign)
- Buildings in the commons area are available from the start and provide basic effects

#### Automa (Solo and Low Player Count)
- Charters not controlled by human players are managed by an Automa system
- Automa players place minion workers on buildings according to simple AI rules
- Automa charters still develop with buildings over the campaign
- The Automa system ensures all 6 charters are active regardless of player count
- At 1-5 players, the unoccupied charters are run by Automa opponents

#### Persona Cards
- Unlocked through crates during the campaign
- Each persona provides a unique ongoing ability for the player who claims it
- Personas may change or be upgraded as the campaign progresses
- They add asymmetry — different players will develop different strategic strengths

#### Between-Game Persistence
- The game board retains all building stickers permanently
- Players keep their charter chests with accumulated cards and some resources
- Influence totals carry over as the campaign score
- New rules from opened crates remain in effect
- Some VP and reputation positions reset at the start of each new game
- The Chronicle booklet accumulates story stickers tracking the narrative

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### End of Game Trigger
Each individual game in the campaign ends when the progress token reaches the end of the progress track. The progress track advances whenever certain actions are taken (primarily constructing buildings and completing guideposts). The number of advancements needed varies by game.

#### In-Game Scoring
Victory points are earned during each game through:
- **Constructing buildings** — Each building grants VP when built, with more advanced buildings worth more
- **Completing guideposts** — Objective cards award VP upon completion
- **Reputation track bonuses** — Reaching certain thresholds on the reputation track awards VP
- **Building activation bonuses** — Some buildings grant VP when workers are placed on them
- **Charter ownership bonuses** — When opponents use buildings in your charter, you gain VP or resources
- **End-of-game scoring** — Some effects grant VP based on conditions met at game end (resources held, buildings constructed, track positions)

#### End of Each Game
1. Score any end-of-game bonuses
2. Record each player's VP total for the game
3. The winner of the individual game earns bonus influence
4. Add VP to each player's cumulative influence total on the campaign tracker
5. Resolve any story/Chronicle events triggered by the game's conclusion
6. Prepare the board state for the next game (some elements reset, board and buildings persist)

#### Campaign Victory (After Game 12)
- The player with the highest cumulative influence across all 12 games wins the campaign
- Influence is the sum of VP earned across all games plus any bonus influence from game wins and special achievements
- Tiebreaker: the player who won more individual games

#### Post-Campaign (Recharge Pack)
- After completing the 12-game campaign, the board is a unique, fully-built worker placement game
- The Recharge Pack is sold separately and allows you to play a second campaign on the reverse side of the board
- The completed campaign board can also be played as a standalone replayable worker placement game with no legacy elements — a permanent custom game unique to your group

#### Typical Scoring
- Early campaign games (Games 1-4): 30-50 VP per game, fewer buildings and options
- Mid campaign (Games 5-8): 50-80 VP per game as engines develop
- Late campaign (Games 9-12): 80-120+ VP per game with fully developed charters
- Campaign totals: 700-1000+ cumulative influence depending on consistency

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Opening Priorities
- **Build in your own charter first.** Constructing buildings in your charter gives you ownership bonuses whenever anyone uses those buildings. Early construction creates a passive VP engine that compounds over the entire campaign.
- **Diversify your resource production.** Make sure you can produce multiple resource types. Being locked out of a key resource stalls your construction plans.
- **Complete guideposts quickly.** Guideposts unlock new content through crates and drive the campaign forward. The first player to complete a guidepost often gets a bonus reward.
- **Use other players' buildings strategically.** You can place on any building on the board. Sometimes an opponent's building offers exactly what you need — just be aware they gain a bonus when you do.
- **Manage your workers carefully.** With only 2 workers at the start, every placement matters. Avoid returning workers too early when there are still good placements available, but don't wait so long that you miss critical buildings.
- **Invest in reputation early.** The reputation track provides VP thresholds and tiebreakers. Falling behind on reputation is hard to recover from.

#### Common Beginner Mistakes
- **Ignoring charter development** — Spending all your time using other players' buildings instead of building your own. Your charter is your long-term engine; invest in it.
- **Hoarding resources** — Resources are meant to be spent on construction. Sitting on a pile of wood and iron while opponents build powerful buildings puts you behind.
- **Neglecting guideposts** — Guideposts are not optional side quests. They drive the story, unlock new mechanics, and award significant VP. Prioritize them.
- **Building randomly** — Place buildings that synergize with each other. A charter full of unrelated buildings is less powerful than one with a coherent resource-to-VP conversion chain.
- **Forgetting the campaign arc** — Charterstone is 12 games. Decisions in Game 1 affect Game 12. Build with the long term in mind — a slightly weaker building now that unlocks future combos is often better than a short-term VP grab.
- **Not reading opened crate content carefully** — New rules from crates are permanent. Missing a key rule change can put you at a disadvantage for multiple games.

#### Campaign-Level Strategy
- **Consistency wins campaigns.** You don't need to win every game — placing in the top half consistently accumulates more influence than winning 3 games and losing 9.
- **Adapt to new mechanics.** Each crate opening changes the game. Players who quickly integrate new rules and buildings into their strategy gain an edge.
- **Watch the influence totals.** Keep an eye on the cumulative campaign score. If you're behind, take more risks in individual games. If you're ahead, play conservatively to protect your lead.
- **Develop a charter identity.** Over the campaign, your charter should develop a clear strategic identity — maybe you're the resource production powerhouse, or the reputation track specialist, or the conversion engine. Lean into your strengths.

subtopics.title: First-Game Walkthrough
subtopics.content:
#### Your First Game of Charterstone — A Guided Walkthrough

**Setup**: Each player opens their charter box and applies the Game 1 stickers. Place one worker on your starting building, keep the second in reserve. Start with 10 VP and basic resources.

**Early Turns (1–5)**
- Use your starting building to collect basic resources (pumpkins, coins, wood)
- Visit the commons buildings for additional actions
- Start saving resources for your first construction

**Mid-Game (6–12)**
- Construct your first building — choose a card from your hand and pay its resource cost
- Place the building sticker in your charter area (this is permanent!)
- Consider building in other players' charters for tactical advantage
- Start working toward guideposts (objectives) for bonus VP

**Late Game (13–end)**
- Focus on scoring: construct high-VP buildings, complete guideposts
- Use the reputation track — higher reputation = more points
- Don't hoard resources; convert everything to VP before the game ends

**After Game 1**
- Open any earned crates and follow the instructions inside
- Record scores on the progress track
- The losing player(s) get a small catch-up bonus for Game 2
- New rules and buildings will be introduced — the game gets richer each session

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Campaign-Level Thinking

**Building Placement is Permanent**: Every building you place stays for all 12 games. Think about which buildings create the best engine for future games, not just the current one. A building that generates pumpkins might win Game 3 but be useless in Games 8–12.

#### Charter Territory Control

**Fill Your Charter First**: Buildings in your charter give you a bonus whenever other players visit. Having 5 strong buildings means opponents feed you resources every time they use your area. Prioritize buildings with broadly useful effects (resource generation, card draw).

#### Reputation vs. Influence

**Reputation** gives end-game VP and ongoing bonuses — high reputation players score more from guideposts. **Influence** determines turn order and breaks ties. In close games, the influence track winner has a huge advantage. Balance both but lean toward reputation early.

#### Crate Optimization

Crates unlock new content and powerful cards. Earning crates earlier means more games with that content. If a guidepost awards a crate, prioritize it over raw VP.

#### Adaptation

The game evolves. Strategies that worked in Games 1–4 may be obsolete by Game 8. Stay flexible, read new cards carefully, and be willing to shift your engine when new mechanics appear.

key_rulings.topic: Workers can be placed on any building
key_rulings.text: On your turn, you may place a worker on any unoccupied building space on the entire board — including buildings in other players' charter areas and the commons.
When another player places a worker on a building in your charter, you receive a small bonus (typically 1 VP or 1 coin). This creates a passive income stream from buildings you construct.
Once a building sticker is placed on the game board, it remains there permanently for all future games in the campaign. Building placement decisions are irreversible.

key_rulings.topic: Returning workers costs a full turn
key_rulings.text: To return all your workers from the board to your supply, you must spend your entire turn. You cannot place a worker and return workers on the same turn.
When a crate is opened, all new rules, resources, and mechanics it introduces are permanent additions to the game. They remain in effect for all subsequent games in the campaign.

key_rulings.topic: Progress track ends the game — finish the round
key_rulings.text: When the progress token advances to the last space, finish the current round so every player has had an equal number of turns. The game does NOT end immediately when the token hits the final space.

The overall campaign winner is the player with the highest cumulative influence across all 12 games. Influence equals total VP earned across all games plus bonus influence from winning individual games.

You can bump any regular worker on the board, even your own. When you place your worker on an occupied building, the existing worker (yours or an opponent's) is returned to its owner's supply. This is a key strategic mechanism.

Ties are consistently 'friendly' in Charterstone. Multiple players can win the game, have the most reputation, or achieve a guidepost simultaneously. There is no tiebreaker — tied players all count as sharing that position.

key_rulings.topic: Crate ownership — your crate regardless of source
key_rulings.text: If you unlock a crate on a constructed building card, no matter how you obtained that card or the content on that card, it is YOUR crate. If the Index Guide header tells you to gain cards from it, YOU gain those cards.

After Game 1, players keep ALL coins, resources, and advancement cards for Game 2. Starting from the end of Game 2 onward, each player discards components down to the Capacity of their Charter Chest. Non-winners fill in additional capacity bubbles as a catch-up mechanism.

key_rulings.topic: Buildings can only be constructed in your charter
key_rulings.text: There is no connection between any building cards and any specific charter. If you have a building card, you can construct it. However, the only eligible location to construct a building is in your own charter's open construction sites.
